speaker: interviewer
text: September 1995 and we're in Sydney, Australia. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] So you found out that Miss Hallingwarth had the car back in London.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She told me that she made a colossal career. So she was by now a very well-known correspondent, syndicated through many, many newspapers in the world. She was, during the war, made a correspondent in the Middle East, and after the war, she became the correspondent in the Far East. Anyway, it was a funny story with Twades. He told me that he can't help me. I said to him, all right, you almost-- we joined actually you and you are morally responsible for this. She was with you. And I said to him, look, I said, I am here with you and I am alive. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The chances were very poor that I ever will be talking to you after the war, so I want from you now-- I-- I-- forget the car. I want a visa to Australia. And he said to me, look, I would give it to you, but it's not in our power. We don't-- we don't have the right to give visas to Australia. Is only the military mission-- Austrian military mission in Berlin which can give you or Austrian Consulate or whatever they are. And he was right. It was like that. What was funny about it, two or three weeks later came a guy who joined us, who knew, not me, but this far relative and a friend of mine who was with me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was a German Jew, who, during the war, was in London working for the intelligence. And he was later parachuted over France, organizing the resistance there. And he came now to Germany to organize the German press in Germany. [SPEAKING GERMAN] He was telling us that he has to see a friend here, a woman from Vienna, who married an English council, Twades. [LAUGHS] It was his second wife, a Jewish woman. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So with him, I saw Twades again. [LAUGHS] Then I found out that Twades left his first wife in Chicago, with children, and he came to Germany. A brother-in-law of Mr. Fierstenfeldt, who was in charge of the camp after the war in Bergen-Belsen, and then he was in charge of the camp in Feldafing. And I met the guy, and somehow talking about something, I don't know how it came out, I found out that he actually took financial care of the wife of Twades. [LAUGHS] This how small the world is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Twades had a hard time whilst he was-- he was going through town with his wife, and they took him for a German, and a drunken American sergeant tried to pick up his wife. So he-- he was not a big guy, so the guy hit him twice and he fell over. It was a diplomatic big story, you know? Then, somebody was shooting over there in the military. They shot a-- a revolver or a rifle, and the bullet came into his office on the third floor, next to his [INAUDIBLE]. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as Miss Hallingwarth was concerned, Clare Hallingwarth, she married the managing director of [INAUDIBLE] Press, and she very well off. And I tried to sue her in London, but because of the English conditions, because I was not a resident of England, I would have had to put a deposit for the cost, and I didn't have the money for it. So I didn't sue her. Long time passed and I was on one of the trade missions, and I found myself in Hong Kong. And somebody told me that Miss Hallingwarth-- someone came out-- in the press club. And I wanted to go and approach her and I phone for her and I was waiting for her to come, so I put the receiver down. And I said, hell, the money doesn't matter to me anymore about the car. [LAUGHS] And if I accuse her, she would deny it. She's popular, world-renowned, very well-known. So I gave the whole story up.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't ask her--

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't go to see her. No, no, because I knew that she will-- she will-- she will tell that she doesn't remember this or not true, that I am lying. What proof do I have? None. [BACKGROUND NOISE] It's her word against mine. And her word will be more held because she was in the milieu of people whom she knew well, and she was well-known, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: --so I didn't see that it was any use of doing it.

speaker: interviewer
text: After all your experiences, do you-- would you like to leave a message for-- for maybe your grandchildren or future generations about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, is one message. I believe that-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't believe in good or bad luck in life. I would say it's a luck of averages, and that we are creating our own luck. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There are people who have constant bad luck, but if you go deeper into it, they are causing it. And the good luck is a similar story. All right, you can have good luck and win lottery when you bought one ticket, yes. Or bad luck-- you can walk and a car can come on the sidewalk and kill you. It's bad luck. But average, doesn't exist. It does not. And I found something else. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] That the biggest trouble among people comes [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] from individuals and the organization who are in charge of different religions. So the religion is a fight for power. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Look, this has nothing to do in believing in God or not believing in God or something. But I didn't see one religion who's also really tolerant. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And there's another position.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for sharing your testimony with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you for being here and for giving me the opportunity. And maybe if I get the copy of this video which you are taking now, I will have something to give to my children and grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this photo, if you could go from left to right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Left is myself, my brother, my mother, and my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And just go through the names, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of my brother is Michael. Name-- name of my mother is Bala, and name of my father is Ludwik.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1934, if I remember right, before my brother went to Palestine to study at the Hebrew University.

speaker: interviewer
text: And whereabouts? In your home or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was, I think, taken at a photographer place.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Sosnowiec?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sosnowiec. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this photo is my mother

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is she?

speaker: interviewee
text: And some friends of hers. She is the one in the last row, looking at the photo, on the-- on the left-hand side.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the left corner

speaker: interviewee
text: A little bit faded. Yes, this was done somewhere-- somehow-- somewhere before the First World War. I would say around 1906, 1907.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I am right, in [INAUDIBLE]. This was a popular [INAUDIBLE] that they were often going. And this was my mother with her friends who were some university students in those days and they wore the uniforms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is myself. I had a motorcycle. I had an army car as well, but I had a motorcycle privately.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This would be taken in 1945, towards the end of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Piastow, not far from Warsaw. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, who is in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This, in this photo is-- is-- the man on the left is a Frenchman. I forgot his name now. He was working with us. I forgot his name. And myself. This was during my work with IRO. This photo must have been taken somewhere in the first part of 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was, I suppose, in [INAUDIBLE] was probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're wearing--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm wearing an American uniform with a so-called Eisenhower stamp, you know, the olive brown--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, with-- with the IRO--

speaker: interviewee
text: IRO, the letters, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Signature on the side.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were so-called members of the voluntary organization. Until '48 it was obligatory for to wear uniforms. Nobody was permitted to wear-- wear civilian clothes--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --for security reasons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife and myself. This was taken in our home in Stamburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ariel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year would that have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What year would that have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: This would be [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1947, I suppose, or 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photo taken at Vatican, when the then-Pope, Paul VI, received the Committee of the International Conference in Endocrinology. My brother was the Chairman of the Mediterranean part of the association.

speaker: interviewer
text: So which one is your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- the-- with the back standing here is the Pope Paul VI. The person to whom he talks is one of the Italians, who was the host of the conference. My brother is first to the left of his-- right of his, actually, whose profile you can see. Then the next to my brother, I don't know the others, is the son of Bloom, who was the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Prime Minister of France before the outbreak of the Second World War.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken-- I don't know the exact date-- Paul VI, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] in the '50s.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's this document here?

speaker: interviewee
text: This a letter written by my brother to our parents, to Warsaw. My brother was in Palestine, in Jerusalem, and this letter went through the International Red Cross. On the top part is the writing-- on this part of my brother on the--

speaker: interviewer
text: And here we can see the date, that it was in 1941.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1941, yes On the other side of the letter, if you turn it over, will be the reply of my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is the other side of the same letter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In this-- this is a reply from my father. In this reply, he's asking, as well, if my brother knows about my whereabouts, if I am alive and what have you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you could go around the table and tell me who's in that photo, maybe starting with yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, sure. Yes, to my left is sitting my younger daughter, Jenniffer. Then is my oldest granddaughter, Lydia, who is now at the university in-- in the third year. Then is my son-in-law, David Bennett. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The next person is a very close friend of the family from Zurich, Switzerland, who is here with her son, who is seen here on the-- in the front of the-- with his back to us. The person with the back to us with the spotted pullover-- or what is I suppose-- is my wife, but you can't see.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the lady on your right?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the lady on the right is my older daughter, Annabelle Bennett. Yes, Annabelle is a barrister QC and Jenniffer is a solicitor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Followed in your footsteps.

speaker: interviewee
text: More or less, yes. Her husband is a barrister, as well as a QC. He is now the President of the Bar Association.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. OK, that's the end of type five of the interview with Emanuel Darin. It's the 18th of September, 1995, and we are in Sydney, Australia. And that's also the end of the whole interview. [INTERPOSING VOICES]
